narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 75.8% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your class rankings from earlier and later years in school, alongside your LSAT performance. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the average of about 6 for students who passed the bar—suggesting to the model that your performance in more advanced coursework raised concerns about handling the rigors of legal studies and the exam. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile of 3, again well below the typical 6 for successful bar takers, reinforcing a pattern of being in the lower third of your class that the model saw as a continued risk. Your LSAT score of 29.3, while not the top driver, actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing, as it exceeded what the model might expect for someone with your other metrics, compared to the average 37.3 among those who passed. Other aspects, like your middle family income bracket—which aligns with over a third of bar passers—and your plan to attend full-time, much like 93% of successful students, played lesser roles and didn't outweigh the academic concerns. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 was slightly below the 3.24 average for passers but wasn't among the top influences here. In essence, the model compared you to thousands of past students and saw your lower deciles as the biggest red flags, unlike accepted applicants who typically ranked much higher in their classes. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven estimate focused purely on bar passage patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that positive influence, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or targeted study, or exploring programs with bridge options for lower-ranked students. You might also gather letters highlighting your resilience or non-academic strengths to supplement future applications. With focused improvements, you can shift these predictions in your favor next time.
